content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 8,
1993
Gene Rogers
Assistant City Manager
City of Moreno Valley
P.O.
Box 1440
Moreno Valley,
CA
92556-1440
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—92—629
Dear Mr.
Rogers:
You have requested advice under the conflict—of—interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) •1/
Since you
are seeking general assistance and not inquiring about a specific
decision before you,
your letter is considered a request for
informal assistance.
2
!
QUESTION
Is it a conflict of interest for you to perform your official
duties as contract administrator for the city if a city contractor
is threatening to sue you and the city regarding the manner in
which the contract was administered?
CONCLUSION
You have not provided any information on any decision before
you regarding this potential litigation which could affect your
economic interests.
Therefore,
we can only provide you with a
general analysis of your question at this time.
1!
Government Code Sections 81000—91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000
g.
All references to regulations are to Title
2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2!
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section
18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Advice File No.
1—92—629
Page Two
FACTS
You are the assistant city manager for the City of Moreno
Valley.
As part of your duties,
you function as the contract
administrator for the city.
The city contracts its animal control
services with a private entity.
That entity is threatening to sue
the city,
and you personally, regarding the manner in which you
administered its contract.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits public officials from making,
participating in,
or using their official position to influence a
governmental decision in which they have a financial interest.
An
official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning
of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its
effect on the public generally,
on the official,
on a member of
the official’s immediate family,
or on any of the financial
interests outlined in Section 87103.
The effect of a governmental
decision is also material if the decision will result in the
personal expenses,
income, assets
(other than interest in real
property),
or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate
family increasing or decreasing by at least $250.
(Regulation
18702.1(a) (4).)
It does not appear that at this point,
the threat of a
lawsuit would have a foreseeable material financial effect on your
personal expenses,
income,
assets or liabilities.
If the animal
control agency proceeds with such a lawsuit,
you may be prohibited
from participating in decisions concerning the lawsuit if it is
foreseeable that you will be affected by $250 or more.
(Dixon
Advice Letter,
No.
A-92-227,
enclosed.)
We suggest you contact us when you have a specific decision
concerning this matter before you.
If you need further
assistance, please feel free to contact the Legal Division at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
Jeanette E. Turvill
Political Reform Consultant
Legal Division
JM/JET/jt
Enclosure
Jeff Marshner
—
